commit.message:
feat(ui): 4K viewport-relative scaling

- html font-size: clamp(12px, 0.8333vw,  all rem() values now32px)
  scale linearly with viewport width (16px at 1920px, 32px at 3840px)
- Remove fixed 1400900 max-size caps from root container; panel now
  fills 100% of available space at any resolution
- Convert .flow-ring-wrap and ::-webkit-scrollbar from px to rem so
  they scale with the root font-size
- HeroSection: RingProgress size derived from viewport width via
  useViewportSize, clamped 240px, thickness proportional to size90

fix: revert position:fixed on app, fix scroll/navbar/planner/settings

Root cause: position:fixed on .app broke scroll in all views, caused
navbar to float high, made touch events require double-tap, and
prevented stats page scrolling.

- Revert .app to static positioning with height:100%, overflow:hidden
- Add overflow:hidden to html/body/root to prevent iOS body bounce
- Add -webkit-overflow-scrolling:touch to .view for iOS momentum scroll
- NavBar: remove translateZ hack (no longer needed)
- NavBar: remove hover state that caused double-tap on mobile
- Planner actions: remove sticky positioning entirely — buttons now
  flow naturally after the day grid, no more Sunday overlap
- Settings: flat list layout — removed card shadows, added subtle
  border, proper spacing between sections

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

feat: close foundation A/B contracts (#109)

This foundation branch closes the remaining Track A and Track B work for
sightjack.

The issue was that sightjack already had the first shared slices for
harness boundaries, provider runtime surfacing, and corrective metadata
forwarding, but those pieces had not yet been closed into one explicit
foundation contract. That meant the shared boundary model was still
split across conformance and semgrep references, and provider state was
not yet consistently preserved through the full corrective feedback and
insight path.

The root cause was that the cross-tool foundation work had progressed
incrementally. Initial slices had landed for status surfacing, retry
telemetry, and corrective metadata forwarding, but the final
contract-closing pass had not yet unified the references, constants, and
insight surfaces that make Track A and Track B complete rather than
partial.

The fix closes that gap. The branch now references the shared semgrep
layer contract from conformance, so the harness boundary story is
explicit and aligned with the actual enforcement rules. It also carries
provider runtime state through corrective metadata and into corrective
insight output, so degraded or waiting provider conditions stay attached
to corrective feedback instead of being visible only in status or retry
telemetry.

The result is that Track A is closed as a shared documented boundary
contract, and Track B is closed as a provider runtime contract that now
survives through sightjack's corrective feedback and insight surfaces.

---------

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

fix: harden improvement metadata intake (#107)

## Summary

This hardens sightjack improvement metadata intake so legacy v1
corrective metadata is accepted during the compatibility window,
severity is preserved, and unsupported outcome vocabulary is rejected
before it pollutes downstream insight handling.

## Problem

The cross-tool improvement rollout introduced a shared metadata
contract, but sightjack still treated missing schema version as
non-improvement input and did not fully normalize the shared severity
and outcome vocabulary. That made older corrective messages easy to drop
and weakened the shared ledger semantics across tools.

## Root cause

The session-layer intake path depended on a strict schema-version
presence check instead of recognizing legacy v1 improvement metadata by
vocabulary. Shared severity and outcome helpers also were not fully
wired through the local metadata and insight path.

## Fix

The domain improvement model now preserves severity, normalizes outcome
vocabulary, accepts recognizable legacy v1 metadata, and propagates the
normalized fields into sightjack correction insight handling. Regression
tests cover round-trip metadata, vocabulary checks, and the legacy
compatibility path.

## Validation

Validated with `go test ./internal/domain ./internal/session`.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
feat: track provider state in session metadata (#106)

## Summary

This PR carries the observable improvement loop forward in sightjack and
makes provider pause state persistent instead of log-only.

It keeps corrective metadata attached to rerun-linked feedback and
reports, and it stores a shared provider state snapshot in coding
session metadata. That gives later runs and external tooling a
consistent view of whether the provider is active, waiting, degraded, or
paused.

## Root cause

Sightjack already had retry, waiting-cycle, and circuit-breaker
behaviors, but those states were not captured in a common
machine-readable shape. Corrective reruns could also carry correlation,
but not the retry and escalation decision context needed for consistent
self-improvement.

## Fix

The change extends shared improvement metadata coverage in tests, adds a
reusable ProviderStateSnapshot contract, exposes circuit breaker
snapshots through that contract, and writes the resulting provider state
into persisted session metadata.

## Validation

- go test ./internal/domain ./internal/session

- go test ./internal/platform ./internal/session -run
"TestSessionTrackingAdapter|TestCircuitBreaker"

---------

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Fix transient error caching, digest slice panic, and file permission overwrite (#41)

Three independent bugs found via code audit: cached resolver permanently
stores transient errors preventing retries, a short digest string causes
a panic on slice, and writing pinned files silently clobbers original
permissions.

## Changes

- **`internal/resolver/resolver.go`** — `CachedResolver.Resolve` and
`CachedResolver.Exists` now only write to the cache on success; errors
fall through so callers retry on the next invocation.

- **`cmd/pin.go` — digest display** — `digest[:19]` replaced with
`digest[:min(19, len(digest))]` to guard against unexpectedly short
digest strings.

- **`cmd/pin.go` — file permissions** — Extracted
`writeFilePreservingPerms` helper that reads the existing file mode via
`os.Stat` before writing, falling back to `0644` only when the stat
fails. Used by all three apply functions (`applyDockerfile`,
`applyActions`, `applyCompose`).

```go
func writeFilePreservingPerms(path string, data []byte) error {
    perm := os.FileMode(0644)
    if fi, err := os.Stat(path); err == nil {
        perm = fi.Mode().Perm()
    }
    return os.WriteFile(path, data, perm)
}
```

## Tests

- **`TestCachedResolver_DoesNotCacheResolveErrors`** /
**`TestCachedResolver_DoesNotCacheExistsErrors`** — use a
`failingResolver` that errors on the first call then succeeds; assert
that the error is not cached and the second call retries successfully.
- **`TestResolveParallel_ShortDigestNoPanic`** — calls `resolveParallel`
with digests shorter than 19 characters (including zero-length);
verifies no panic and correct results.
- **`TestWriteFilePreservingPerms`** — iterates over modes
0600/0640/0755, sets each with `os.Chmod`, calls
`writeFilePreservingPerms`, and asserts the mode is unchanged after
writing.
- **`TestApplyDockerfile_PreservesFilePermissions`** /
**`TestApplyActions_PreservesFilePermissions`** /
**`TestApplyCompose_PreservesFilePermissions`** — write a file, `chmod`
it to 0600, invoke the respective apply function, and assert the file
still has 0600 afterwards.

fix #40

---------

Co-authored-by: copilot-swe-agent[bot] <198982749+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>
Co-authored-by: azu <19714+azu@users.noreply.github.com>
Co-authored-by: devin-ai-integration[bot] <158243242+devin-ai-integration[bot]@users.noreply.github.com>

feat: record corrective metadata as insights (#105)

## Summary

This changeset makes sightjack consume the minimal corrective metadata
contract and project it into insights.

Sightjack could already receive feedback D-Mails and react to them in
the waiting and rescan loop, but the structured corrective metadata
introduced for the MVP loop was not being persisted anywhere on the
consumer side. That meant the loop was observable at send time but not
yet at receive time.

This PR adds the shared improvement metadata types to sightjack, extends
feedback collection with a lightweight hook, and writes
correction-derived entries into `improvement-loop.md` in the insight
ledger whenever corrective D-Mails are received. The existing D-Mail
processing path remains intact; this is additive observability and
learning-oriented projection.

The effect is that sightjack now preserves structured corrective context
from incoming feedback, which helps complete the first end-to-end
observable improvement loop.

## Validation

- go test ./internal/domain ./internal/session

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>
